_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 1
questions.prompt: Which declaration lets a class promise the methods in Payable?
questions.explanation: The `implements` keyword connects a class to an interface contract. The class must then provide the required abstract methods.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 2
choices: It asks the compiler to verify a matching parent method
questions.explanation: The annotation catches misspelled names and mismatched parameters at compile time. It does not change access or create inheritance by itself.

questions.title: Trace Inheritance & Interfaces
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Both classes satisfy Payable, so one list can hold both. The loop calls the version belonging to the actual object. The resulting output is '25.0\n10.0'.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 5
questions.prompt: Why declare the parameter as the interface `Payable` rather than the class `Invoice`?
choices: The method then accepts any type that implements Payable, including future ones
Interfaces run faster than concrete classes
Invoice cannot be printed with println
Only interfaces are allowed as method parameters
questions.explanation: Accepting `Payable` lets the method work with `Invoice`, `Receipt`, or any class that implements the interface, now or later. Requiring `Invoice` locks it to one class. This is about flexibility, not speed, and concrete classes are perfectly valid parameter types too.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Inheritance means is-a"?
choices: Use `extends` only when every child truly is a usable version of the parent. If one class merely needs another object's help, keep that helper in a field and use composition.
questions.explanation: A class `extends` one superclass and may `implement` several interfaces. Override methods to provide subtype behavior, use the interface type for flexible variables, and prefer composition when classes only need to reuse a helper rather than form a true is-a relationship. That reasoning supports the answer: Use `extends` only when every child truly is a usable version of the parent. If one class merely needs another object's help, keep that helper in a field and use composition.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable Inheritance & Interfaces practice?
choices: Use an interface to state a capability.
questions.explanation: A class `extends` one superclass and may `implement` several interfaces. Override methods to provide subtype behavior, use the interface type for flexible variables, and prefer composition when classes only need to reuse a helper rather than form a true is-a relationship. That reasoning supports the answer: Use an interface to state a capability.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of Inheritance & Interfaces code?
choices: Type variables by the contract when callers need flexibility.
questions.explanation: A class `extends` one superclass and may `implement` several interfaces. Override methods to provide subtype behavior, use the interface type for flexible variables, and prefer composition when classes only need to reuse a helper rather than form a true is-a relationship. That reasoning supports the answer: Type variables by the contract when callers need flexibility.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common Inheritance & Interfaces mistake?
questions.explanation: A class `extends` one superclass and may `implement` several interfaces. Override methods to provide subtype behavior, use the interface type for flexible variables, and prefer composition when classes only need to reuse a helper rather than form a true is-a relationship. That reasoning supports the answer: Use `@Override` for compiler checking.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of Inheritance & Interfaces. Which advice is correct?
choices: Reserve `extends` for a true is-a relationship.
questions.explanation: A class `extends` one superclass and may `implement` several interfaces. Override methods to provide subtype behavior, use the interface type for flexible variables, and prefer composition when classes only need to reuse a helper rather than form a true is-a relationship. That reasoning supports the answer: Reserve `extends` for a true is-a relationship.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces concept check 6
questions.prompt: Which choice shows sound reasoning about Inheritance & Interfaces?
choices: Prefer composition for simple code reuse.
questions.explanation: A class `extends` one superclass and may `implement` several interfaces. Override methods to provide subtype behavior, use the interface type for flexible variables, and prefer composition when classes only need to reuse a helper rather than form a true is-a relationship. That reasoning supports the answer: Prefer composition for simple code reuse.
